I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:

I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the facility. No guarantee has been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released when authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.

Il. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:

I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician assistants) who are not employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III, V, and VI below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians, practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.

Ill. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:

I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as requested by such physician(s). I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the completion of any claim for benefits arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I authorize the release of my HIV test results to healthcare personnel in the event of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the company and/or its workers compensation administrator as indicated below:

THE FACILITY IS NOT RESPONSIBLE FOR LOSS OF PERSONAL ARTICLES:

I understand that the facility maintains a safe for the safekeeping of money and valuables and the facility shall not be liable for the loss of or damage to

any money, jewelry, documents, or any other items of personal property, unless deposited in the facility safe for safekeeping. Furthermore, I accept full

responsibility for items of personal property kept in my possession.

V. ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

I agree to the assignment of all third-party benefits to the facility and agree to pay the facility for all charges not covered by third-party payors. In the

case of outpatient services I agree that this document shall remain in full force and effect until specifically revoked by me in writing. Any sums not paid

by a third-party payor are my obligation.

VI. RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:

I agree to the release by my insurance carrier to the facility and the facility's billing service of any eligibility and utilization data concerning my insurance

coverage which the facility and/or its billing supplier may require. I authorize the facility to file an appeal of a payment denial and/or request a hearing

regarding any denial of payment for treatment provided to me. I further authorize the facility to present proof, utilize facility records and my records

and documentation for appeals and hearings.

VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:

I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and

terms of the facility. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its third-party

administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of bills are payable at the end of each week and in full

upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an

attorney for collection. Should it be necessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to pay the costs of collection,

including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.

I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call or text the cell number I provide for any reason, including but

not limited to inquiring about the status and collection of my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but

not limited to automatic dialing, artificial and prerecorded voice systems.

HIPAA COMPLIANT MEDICAL AUTHORIZATION

(Authorization to Release Protected Health Care Information)

To: Norton Brownsboro Hospital 4960 Norton Healthcare Blvd. Louisville, KY 40241

Dear Medical Records Clerk:

I have retained The Whaley Law Firm, PSC to represent me in matters related to an injury sustained due to a third parties’ negligence. Please forward a copy of any & all records not previously provided to The Whaley Law Firm upon request.

Pursuant to the Health Insurance Portability & Accountability Act (HIPAA) Privacy Regulations, 45 CFR 164.508, the provider listed above is hereby authorized to release to The Whaley Law Firm, PSC or any of their representatives, all medical records, including but not limited to: Office Notes, History, Physical, Consultation Notes, Discharge Summaries, Orders, Progress Notes, Laboratory Results, Nurses’ Notes, Emergency Room Records, Operative Reports, In-Patient Records and films of X-Rays, MRIs, CT Scans, or PET Scans, Pharmacy and Drug Records, Medical Bills and Health Insurance Information, Medicaid/Medicare Records, concerning ANY medical treatment that I have received from you and/or at your facility, as well as ALL such records which you keep in the regular course of business that are found in my medical record file.

I hereby authorize the release of ANY & ALL records regarding mental health, psychiatric (other than psychotherapy notes, which must be requested under separate authorization), Chemical Dependency or HIV.

A photostatic copy hereof shall be as valid as the original.

I hereby authorize a FREE COPY of my medical records pursuant to KRS 422.317 be sent to the extent I have not already requested my one free copy.

The purpose of this authorization and request is to permit my attorney to obtain ALL medical information pertaining to my physical and mental condition(s). This authorization expires in three (3) years from the date of the signature. The aforementioned expiration date has not passed, as this matter is on-going. I hereby authorize The Whaley Law Firm, PSC. to speak to my healthcare providers privately or to take the testimony at deposition and/or trial as may be requested.

I have the right to revoke this authorization in writing by providing a signed, written notice of revocation to the healthcare provider listed above and to The Whaley Law Firm, PSC. I understand that there are exceptions to the right to revoke. Among the exceptions to the right to revoke are that an individual may not revoke an authorization to the extent that the covered entity has taken action in reliance thereon. Medical Providers may not condition treatment or payment on whether the above-listed patient executes this authorization. The information disclosed pursuant to this authorization may be subject to re-disclosure and no longer protected by the privacy regulations promulgated pursuant to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)

RADIOLOGY REPORT

FACILITY: NORTON BROWNSBORO HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: J.MRIHOS OP AGE:58 Y SEX:M PATIENT NAME/DOB: VOLK, DANIEL, W [DOB-1]

UNIT NUMBER: EP01968571

ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10130164327 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPJ22MRI362076

MRI brain with contrast, brain laboratory protocol, [DOB-1299] at 0654 hours

HISTORY: Right parietal extra-axial mass.

TECHNIQUE: Multiple thin axial T2 and post enhanced axial T1-weighted images were obtained of the brain on the 3 Tesla magnet. FLAIR imaging was performed.

COMPARISON: MRI of the brain performed on January 7, 2022.

FINDINGS: There has been no interval change in the right posterior parafalcine meningioma. This measures 2.1 x 2.1 cm. This was 2.0 x 2.0 cm. Minimal surrounding edema is demonstrated.

There is no evidence of hydrocephalus. The ventricles are normal size and shape.

Flow is seen within the vertebral basilar and carotid arteries. There is mild-to-moderate bilateral maxillary mucosal thickening. Scattered opacification is noted in the ethmoid air cells. Mild-to-moderate mucosal thickening is seen in the frontal and sphenoid sinuses. No mastoid or middle ear effusion is demonstrated.

IMPRESSION:

1. This is the BrainLAB protocol for preoperative planning

2. No interval change in the right parafalcine parietal meningioma.

2. Mild to moderate chronic pansinusitis

Dictated by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.

Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Durrett Craddock, M.D.

V. ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

VI. RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:

VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:

I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and

terms of the facility. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its third-party

administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of bills are payable at the end of each week and in full

upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an

including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.

I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call or text the cell number I provide for any reason, including but

MEDICATION GUIDE CLARISCAN (kla-ri'-skan) (gadoterate meglumine) Injection for intravenous use

What is Clariscan?

* — Clariscan is a prescription medicine calied a gadolinium-based contrast agent (GBCA). Clariscan, like other GBCAs, is injected into your vein and used with a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scanner.

e An MRI exam with a GBCA, including Clariscan, helps your doctor to see problems better than an MRI exam without a GBCA.

e Your doctor has reviewed your medical records and has determined that you would benefit from using a GBCA with your MRI exam.

What is the most important information | should know about Clariscan?

Clariscan contains a metal called gadolinium. Small amounts of gadolinium can Stay i in your body including the brain, bones, skin and other parts of your body for a long time (several months to years).

® _ It is not known how gadolinium may affect you, but so far, studies have not found harmful effects in patients with normal kidneys.

* _ Rarely patients have reported pains, tiredness, and skin, muscle or bone ailments for a long time, but these symptoms have not been directly linked to gadolinium. :

« — There are different GBCAs that can be used for your MRI exam. The amount of gadolinium that stays in the body is different for different gadolinium medicines. Gadolinium stays in the body more after Omniscan or Optimark than after Eovist, Magnevist or MultiHance. Gadolinium ‘stays in the body the least after Clariscan, Dotarem, Gadavist or ProHance.

« ~ People who get many doses of gadolinium medicines, women who are pregnant and young children may be at increased risk from gadolinium staying in the body.

* — Some people with kidney problems who get gadolinium medicines can develop a condition with severe thickening of the skin, muscles and other organs in the body (nephrogenic systemic fibrosis). Your healthcare provider should screen you to see how well your kidneys are working before you receive Clariscan.

Do not receive Clariscan if you have had a severe allergic reaction to Clariscan.

Before receiving Clariscan, tell your healthcare provider about all your medical conditions, including if you:

e — Have had any MRI procedures in the past where you received a GBCA. Your healthcare provider may ask you for more information including the dates of these MRI procedures.

«Are pregnant or pian to become pregnant. It is not known if Clariscan can harm your unborn baby. Talk to your healthcare provider about the possible risks to an unborn baby if a GBCA such as Clariscan is received during pregnancy.

* . Have kidney problems, diabetes, or high blood pressure.

« Have had an allergic reaction to dyes (contrast agents) including GBCAs.

What are possible side effects of Clariscan?

© See "What is the most important information | should know about Clariscan?" ¢ Allergic reactions. Clariscan can cause allergic reactions that can sometimes be serious. Your healthcare provider will monitor you closely for symptoms of an allergic reaction.

The most common side effects of Clariscan include: nausea, headache, pain, or cold feeling at the injection site, and rash.

These are not all the possible side effects of Clariscan. Call your doctor for medical advice about side effects. You may report side effects to FDA at 1-800-FDA-1088.

General information about the safe and effective uses of Clariscan. Medicines are sometimes prescribed for purposes other than those listed in a Medication Guide. You can ask your healthcare provider for information about Clariscan that is written for health professionals.

What are the ingredients in Clariscan? Active ingredient: gadoterate meglumine Inactive ingredients: DOTA, water for injection

Manufactured by: GE Healthcare AS Oslo, Norway

GE and the GE Monogram are trademarks of General Electric Company.

For more information, go to www.GEHealthcare.com or call 1-[PHONE-1314].

This Medication Guide has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. 08/2020

Future Orders

After discharge call doctor for (specify) Ambulatory referral to Physical Therapy Ambulatory referral to Physical Therapy

Call doctor for: chills

Call doctor for: elevated temperature

Call doctor for: headache or visual disturbances Call doctor for: persistent nausea or vomiting Call doctor for: Potential signs of infection Call doctor for: severe uncontrolled pain Consistent carbohydrate diet

Discharge instructions (specify)

Do not drive a car

Do not resume sexual intercourse

Incision / Wound care (specify)

Keep dressing clean and dry

May bathe in tub

May shower

No heavy lifting

No swimming

FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENT - Please call [PHONE-1402] to make and/or confirm your follow-up appointment.

BRAIN SURGERY INCISION CARE - Wash your hair and gently massage the incision daily with baby shampoo. - Do not use any creams or ointments. - Do not blow dry, color or chemically treat your hair for at least 6 months.

DISCHARGE ACTIVITY - Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment within 24 hours of using pain meds. - If you have had seizures, do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until cleared by your surgeon. - No lifting > 10 Ibs. (i.e. one gallon of milk) or strenuous activity until cleared by your surgeon. - Light walking is encouraged - If you were issued a cervical collar, then wear it at all times - If you were issued a back brace, then wear it when sitting up greater than 45 degrees or standing

DISCHARGE DIET

- Low sugar diet (No simple sugars, fruit juices, sweetened beverages, sweets made with sugar) while on steroids.

- Vitamin C supplement over the counter (500 mg daily for 30 days, 250 mg daily thereafter), if you are a current or recent smoker.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

- The nurses will give you a list of your new medications and home medication to resume.

- A limited supply of pain medication will be provided by your surgeon Take only as needed. Pain medication refills will only be considered by your surgeon after you have been seen on your scheduled routine follow-up visit. | have counseled the patient on the risks and benefits of narcotic use and patient has verbalized understanding.

- Prescription refills are completed during normal weekday office hours only.

CALL THE OFFICE AT [PHONE-1403] FOR: - A fever of greater than 101.5 measured with a thermometer.

[DOB-1404] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued) Discharge Summary Note (continued)

- Swelling, tenderness, redness or drainage from your incision.

- Changes in level of awareness (restlessness, confusion, agitation, lethargy or increased sleepiness)

- New speech or swallowing problems

- New weakness, especially one-sided

- Changes in vision

- Persistent vomiting

- Severe headache, especially if worsening

- Seizures or tremors

- Leg swelling with calf tenderness

- If you think you are having an emergency, call 9-1-1. If possible, proceed to the hospital where your surgery was performed.

[DOB-1425] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued) OP Notes (continued)

Fluids/Drains: None

Indications: Daniel W Volk is a 58 yr/o male who presented with imaging demonstrated right parietal lobe mass. | had a detailed conversation with the patient and family regarding potential benefits, risks and alternative to the procedure listed above. They consented to the procedure.

Operation: The patient was positively identified and marked in the preoperative holding room. He was transferred to the operating room and placed under general endotracheal anesthesia. All appropriate monitoring devices were placed. The patient was then positioned in the Mayfield pin head holder. All pressure points were adequately padded. The stereotactic navigation system was then registered to the patient's head and utilized to plan an incision and craniotomy. DVT prophylaxis was provided. Preoperative antibiotics were administered. The patient's head was prepped with alcohol followed by chlorhexidine followed by ChloraPrep solution and draped in sterile fashion. A preoperative timeout was performed.

The location of the incision was confirmed again with the stereotactic navigation system. Local anesthetic was administered. Using a 10 blade scalpel incision was fashioned. Hemostasis was obtained utilizing Raney clips and bipolar electrocautery. The scalp was retracted in a subperiosteal fashion. Using the stereotactic navigation system to confirm the appropriate location of burr holes and using the Midas drill we fashioned a craniotomy without complication. The bone dust was washed away with copious amounts of antibiotic irrigation. Hemostasis was obtained along the bone edges with bone wax and thrombin- soaked Gelfoam. We then brought the stereotactic navigation system back into the field to confirm the extent of the bony exposure. The ultrasound was used to further confirm the exposure.

Next the dura was incised and reflected to expose the underlying brain. We again interrogated lesion with the ultrasound. At this point the microscope was brought into the field and the resection of the lesion was performed under microscope

Generated on 12/6/23 1:25 PM Page 66




IN NORTON NORTON BROWNSBORO Volk, Daniel W

HEALTHCARE HOSPITAL MRN: EP01968571, DOB: 2/22/1964, Sex: M Real people. Remarkable care 4960 Norton Healthcare Blvd Acct #: 216805010 : : Louisville KY 40241-2831 Adm: 6/21/2022, D/C: 6/23/2022

[DOB-1426] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued) OP Notes (continued)

magnification utilizing microsurgical dissection technique. Utilizing tumor forceps, Rhoton dissectors and cottonoids, we worked circumferentially around the margin of the lesion. Hemostasis was obtained with bipolar electrocautery and thrombin-soaked cotton sponges. As we identified the tumor margin, we debulked the lesion internally. The capsule was then dissected further off of the brain and folded inward. We used the stereotactic navigation system as well as ultrasound throughout the extent of the resection for guidance.

When satisfied with the extent of resection we turned our attention to hemostasis which was obtained utilizing bipolar electrocautery, Gelfoam, and thrombin- soaked cottonoids. A dura matrix dural onlay substitute was placed. The bone was reapproximated with a cranial plating system. The wound was then copiously irrigated with antibiotic irrigation. The scalp was then reapproximated in layers with 2-0 Vicryl sutures followed by a 4-0 Monocryl running suture. The patient's hair was then washed with sterile water and chlorhexidine and dressed with ChloraPrep solution and bacitracin ointment. The patient was removed from Mayfield pin headholder. All counts were correct. There are no acute complications.

Electronically signed by Sun, David A., MD at 6/21/2022 12:27 PM

Pre-Procedure Instructions Rose, Dana, RN at 5/23/2022 0940

Author: Rose, Dana, RN Service: — Author Type: Registered Nurse Filed: 5/23/2022 9:41 AM Date of Service: 5/23/2022 9:40 AM Status: Signed Editor: Rose, Dana, RN (Registered Nurse)

Pre-Operative Instructions & Information - Neuro - Dr. Sun

Your surgery is scheduled on __6/21___at-:

Norton Brownsboro Hospital 4960 Norton Healthcare Blvd. Louisville, KY 40241 [PHONE-1450]

Main Entrance

Your To Do List:

Instructions for any of the following medications or any other blood thinners should be discussed with your neurosurgeon prior to surgery.

e NSAIDS (Advil, Aleve, Motrin, Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Meloxicam, Diclofenac oral and gel, Celebrex or Aspirin)

e Antiplatelet medications (Plavix and Brilinta)

° Anticoagulation medications (Coumdain, Pradaxa, Eliquis, Xarelto, Lovenox)

Covid Test: Not needed, vaccinated

Nasal Ointment * A prescription for Bactroban (Mupirocin) Nasal Ointment will be sent to your pharmacy prior to surgery to help prevent infection after surgery. * Use the Nasal Ointment 2 times per day (morning and night) for 5 days prior to surgery. * Put a pea size amount on a Q-Tip and swab inside each nostril. * If redness, irritation or medication allergy or intolerance of ointment occurs to notify their surgeon and stop use of the ointment immediately.

Shower *A bottle of Dyna Hex Soap has been included in your packet. An infection after surgery can cause serious problems with your recovery. Cleaning your skin before surgery with Dyna Hex Soap can lower your chance of an infection.

Generated on 12/6/23 1:25 PM Page 82




IN NORTON NORTON BROWNSBORO Volk, Daniel W

HEALTHCARE HOSPITAL MRN: EP01968571, DOB: 2/22/1964, Sex: M Real’ people; Remarkable care 4960 Norton Healthcare Blvd Acct #: 216805010 peeps Louisville KY 40241-2831 Adm: 6/21/2022, D/C: 6/23/2022

[DOB-1451] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued) Case 1832181 (RIGHT CRANIOTOMY FOR TUMOR) (continued)

* Use your regular soap first. Shower with Dyna Hex Soap second. Leave on skin for 1 min before you rinse. * Keep Dyna Hex soap out of eyes, ears, nose, and mouth and away from genital area.

* Stop using Dyna Hex Soap if you are allergic and switch to Dial antibacterial soap.

* After cleaning with Dyna Hex Soap, rinse well with warm water & pat dry.

* Always apply soap with a clean washcloth and dry off with a clean towel.

Fasting *Do not eat any solid food 8 hours prior to your scheduled ARRIVAL time. Clear, see-through liquids

(water, BLACK coffee, gatorade, ect.) are acceptable up until 2 hours prior to your scheduled ARRIVAL time. No dairy (milk, creamer,shakes, ect.). Avoid candy, gum and mints. “Failure to follow these instructions can lead to an increased risk of lung complications and may result in a delay or cancellation of your procedure.

YOUR SCHEDULE:

5 Days Prior to Surgery *Start Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

4 Days Prior to Surgery *Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

3 Days Prior to Surgery *Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

2 Days Prior to Surgery *Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

1 Day Prior to Surgery *Finish Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower at Night with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel. *Put on clean Pjs & sleep on clean sheets. *Do not smoke or chew tobacco after midnight. *Remove all jewelry (including wedding rings) & leave at home.

Day of Surgery *Shower right before leaving for the hospital with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth & towel. *Do not use mascara, lotions, powder, make-up, perfume, or aftershave. *Bring CPAP, inhalers, glasses (no contacts), dentures & hearing aids (will be removed before surgery). *Don't wear jewelry and remove body piercing. *Bring picture ID, insurance card & copayment if needed. *Do not bring money or valuables, unless you've been told you have a co-pay. *Leave your luggage in the car. Your family will be responsible for your belongings.

MEDICATION INSTRUCTIONS PER ANESTHESIA GUIDELINES:

*Use respiratory inhalers/nebulizer on day of surgery as you normally would.

“You may take Tylenol for pain and headache.

*Continue all other medications not listed above until the night before surgery.*

ALSO...

*Masks are required to enter our buildings.

*Bring your respiratory inhaler and CPAP machine into the hospital when you arrive on surgery day. *You must have someone available to drive you home after your surgery.

*For general anesthesia patients: It is requested by our anesthesia providers that you have someone available to stay with you for 24 hours post operatively.

*Notify your doctor of any changes in health such as fever, cold, flu as well as any changes to skin such as rash, open sores.

*Your surgeon's office will call you with a time to arrive/time of surgery 2-3 days prior to your surgery date.

Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)

Returning at 11:04 per OT request as pt with concerns regarding stair negotiation. Pt performing bed mobility with independence and increased time for supine to sit. Cues for sit to stand and pt able to perform with supervision. Gait training in hall way CGA progressing to supervision x 120' with cues for normalized arm swing, relaxation of upper traps and to increase cadence. Improved from previous session. Step training performed with L handrail and 15 steps. Pt able to utilize reciprocal pattern in both directions with increased time. Returning to supine due to pt fatigue. Increased education to pt as pt with inquiries regarding d/c recs, different settings of PT. All questions answered prior to PT departure. PT signing off as pt has met all goals and is safe to d/c home at this time.

Madeline Ratliff, PT

Electronically signed by Ratliff, Madeline, PT at 6/22/2022 1:16 PM

Plan of Care Kopp, Kellie, RN at 6/21/2022 0908 Author: Kopp, Kellie, RN Service: — Author Type: Registered Nurse Filed: 6/21/2022 9:09 AM Date of Service: 6/21/2022 9:08 AM Status: Signed

Editor: Kopp, Kellie, RN (Registered Nurse)

Met with patient and wife in pre-op. Patient identified by stated name and arm band. Pt is scheduled to have a crani by Dr. Sun this am. Navigator introduced self and role with the brain tumor team. Patient alert and oriented. Navigator reviewed what to expect in hospital after crani. Pt will go to ICU for close monitoring. He will have a post-op MRI overnight. Navigator also reviewed post-op craniotomy surgery home care instructions, what to report, diet and appropriate activity post-surgery. Encouraged patient to eat a no-added sugar diet while on steroids and to continue a healthy diet of fruits and vegetables, protein, dairy and drink plenty of fluids daily. Signs and symptoms of infection reviewed. Provided patient with post-op follow-up appointment with Dr. Sun in the BTC on Monday 7/11. Explained to patient and wife that he will be presented that morning in Neuro Tumor Board where his scans, path and plan of care would be discussed by multiple providers. Also explained to the patient that he would likely be seen by multiple providers during his post-op visit, including the surgeon, PT/OT, speech pathologist, the navigator as well as other physicians as needed. Provided contact information and encouraged patient and family to call with questions or concerns. Provided pt with decadron weaning worksheet and explained how to use for taper. Understanding verbalized. All questions answered appropriately.

The following post-op instructions were reviewed and paper copy provided:

Craniotomy Discharge Instructions Follow-up Appointment e Your follow-up appointment is scheduled on Monday 7/11 at 10:45am at the Brain Tumor Center, Norton Cancer Institute Office, Brownsboro, 4955 Norton Healthcare Blvd, 2"¢ Floor. e Please call [PHONE-1511] to confirm. Incision Care e Wash your hair and gently massage the incision daily with baby shampoo. Use baby shampoo for 6 months. No hair products for 6 weeks. e Donot use any creams or ointments. e Do not blow dry, color or chemically treat your hair for at least 6 months. Discharge Activity e Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment within 24 hours of using pain meds.

Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)

e If you have had seizures, do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until cleared by your surgeon.

e Nolifting > 10 Ibs. (i.e. one gallon of milk) or strenuous activity until cleared by your surgeon.

e Light walking is encouraged.

Discharge Diet

e Low sugar diet (No simple sugars, fruit juices, sweetened beverages, sweets made with sugar) while on steroids.

e Daily multi-vitamin with minerals.

e Vitamin C supplement (500 mg daily for 30 days, 250 mg daily thereafter), if you are a current or recent smoker.

Discharge Medications

e The nurses will give you a list of your new medications and home medication to resume.

e Alimited supply of pain medication will be provided at discharge. Take only as needed. Tylenol/acetaminophen may be taken in place of narcotic pain meds. Do not mix pain meds unless advised by physician or nurse. Pain medication refills will only be considered by Dr. Sun after you have been seen on your scheduled routine follow-up visit.

e Prescription refills are completed during normal weekday office hours only.

Please call the office at [PHONE-1513] for: **Do Not Use MyChart messaging for any post-op issues or needs, call the above number e A fever of greater than 101.5, measured with a thermometer. Swelling, tenderness, redness or drainage from your incision. Changes in level of awareness (restlessness, confusion, agitation, lethargy or increased sleepiness) New speech or swallowing problems New weakness, especially one-sided Changes in vision Persistent vomiting Severe headache, especially if worsening Seizures or tremors Leg swelling with calf tenderness If you think you are having an emergency, call 9-1-1. If possible, proceed to the hospital where your surgery was performed.

**Fatigue and lethargy are common after this procedure. This may last up to 6 weeks following surgery.

**For further questions or concerns, please contact: e Kellie Kopp, RN, Patient Navigator [PHONE-1514] e Karen Richardson, MA [PHONE-1515]

Your To Do List:

Instructions for any of the following medications or any other blood thinners should be discussed with your neurosurgeon prior to surgery.

e NSAIDS (Advil, Aleve, Motrin, Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Meloxicam, Diclofenac oral and gel, Celebrex or Aspirin)

e Antiplatelet medications (Plavix and Brilinta)

° Anticoagulation medications (Coumdain, Pradaxa, Eliquis, Xarelto, Lovenox)

Covid Test: Not needed, vaccinated

Nasal Ointment * A prescription for Bactroban (Mupirocin) Nasal Ointment will be sent to your pharmacy prior to surgery to help prevent infection after surgery. * Use the Nasal Ointment 2 times per day (morning and night) for 5 days prior to surgery. * Put a pea size amount on a Q-Tip and swab inside each nostril. * If redness, irritation or medication allergy or intolerance of ointment occurs to notify their surgeon and stop use of the ointment immediately.

Shower *A bottle of Dyna Hex Soap has been included in your packet. An infection after surgery can cause serious problems with your recovery. Cleaning your skin before surgery with Dyna Hex Soap can lower your chance of an infection. * Use your regular soap first. Shower with Dyna Hex Soap second. Leave on skin for 1 min before you rinse. * Keep Dyna Hex soap out of eyes, ears, nose, and mouth and away from genital area. * Stop using Dyna Hex Soap if you are allergic and switch to Dial antibacterial soap. * After cleaning with Dyna Hex Soap, rinse well with warm water & pat dry. * Always apply soap with a clean washcloth and dry off with a clean towel.

Fasting *Do not eat any solid food 8 hours prior to your scheduled ARRIVAL time. Clear, see-through liquids (water, BLACK coffee, gatorade, ect.) are acceptable up until 2 hours prior to your scheduled ARRIVAL time. No dairy (milk, creamer,shakes, ect.). Avoid candy, gum and mints. “Failure to follow these instructions can lead to an increased risk of lung complications and may result in a delay or cancellation of your procedure.

YOUR SCHEDULE:

5 Days Prior to Surgery *Start Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

4 Days Prior to Surgery *Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

3 Days Prior to Surgery *Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

2 Days Prior to Surgery *Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower anytime with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel.

1 Day Prior to Surgery *Finish Bactroban (mupirocin) Nasal Ointment. Once in the Morning & Once at Night. *Shower at Night with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth and towel. *Put on clean Pjs & sleep on clean sheets. *Do not smoke or chew tobacco after midnight. *Remove all jewelry (including wedding rings) & leave at home.

Day of Surgery *Shower right before leaving for the hospital with Dyna Hex Soap. Use clean washcloth & towel. *Do not use mascara, lotions, powder, make-up, perfume, or aftershave. *Bring CPAP, inhalers, glasses (no contacts), dentures & hearing aids (will be removed before surgery). *Don't wear jewelry and remove body piercing. *Bring picture ID, insurance card & copayment if needed. *Do not bring money or valuables, unless you've been told you have a co-pay. *Leave your luggage in the car. Your family will be responsible for your belongings.

MEDICATION INSTRUCTIONS PER ANESTHESIA GUIDELINES:

ONLY TAKE these medications the morning of surgery with a small sip of water:

STOP these medications:

weeks prior to surgery: Il decongestants (example: Sudafed), Afrin Spray, Alka Seltzer, Pepto Bismol, diet pills, itamins and herbal supplements. days prior to surgery: Marijuana, CBD and Cannabinoid use.

NONE

Bachelors of science degree. Retired from Federal law enforcement last year.

Patient tolerated treatment well

Through administration of standardized assessment with interpretation of scores in combination with additional areas of assessment and reporting of evaluation Pt is deemed to have a cognitive ability WFL. Receptive and expressive language abilities were WFL. Motor speech abilities were deemed to be WEL. At end of evaluation, Pt and family reported Pt is at or very close to baseline. Reported difficulty recalling the name of pain medication (morphine) and occasionally repeating himself. Reviewed impact of lack of sleep, pain medication, and steroids on cognitive function. ST services are not warranted at this time. PT to f/u in OP tumor clinic.

RADIOLOGY REPORT FACILITY: NORTON BROWNSBORO HOSPITAL UNIT/AGE/GENDER: J.PERIHO IN AGE:58 Y SEX:M PATIENT NAME/DOB: VOLK, DANIEL, W [DOB-1] UNIT NUMBER: EP01968571 ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10130165705 ACCESSION NUMBER: EPJ22MRI523412 EXAMINATION: MRI BRAIN WITHOUT AND WITH CONTRAST. DATE: [DOB-1536] INDICATION: Brain/CNS neoplasm, assess treatment response COMPARISON: June 6, 2022 PROCEDURE: Multiplanar multisequence brain MRI without and with intravenous contrast was performed. FINDINGS: The patient is postop parietal craniotomy. There is gross total resection of the parietal meningioma. Mild enhancement of the dura is noted. There is no acute hemorrhage, or infarct. Trace vasogenic edema seen within the right parietal lobe was present on the preoperative exam. There is minimal fluid at the site of craniotomy. The craniotomy flap is in good position. Small bilateral symmetric subgaleal fluid collection.

The remainder of the examination is stable. The intracranial arteries are patent. There is chronic attenuation of the superior sagittal sinus at the site of the meningioma.

Partially empty sella. Negative pineal gland. Scattered mild sinus mucosal thickening and small mucus retention cyst. CONCLUSION: No acute postoperative abnormality following gross total resection of the right parietal meningioma. Dictated by: John Burger, M.D.

Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: John Burger, M.D.

SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT Norton Brownsboro Hospital

Louisville, KY 40241

Patient Name: VOLK, DANIEL W. Accession #: S$22-21 014 DOB: 2/22/1964 (Age: 58) SEX: M MRN:: EP01968571 Location: NBH 3 West Med/Surg Collected: 6/21/2022 Physician(s): DAVID A. SUN MD Received: 6/21/2022

Reported: 6/28/2022

DIAGNOSIS:

A-B. DURA, RIGHT PARIETAL, RESECTION: MENINGIOMA, WHO GRADE 1. SEE SYNOPTIC REPORT AND COMMENT.

SYNOPTIC REPORT:

Report Name: CNS - Histological Assessment Status: Submitted Checklist Inst: 1 Last Updated By: HILARY HIGHFIELD MD, [DOB-1559] 12:14:09

Part(s) Involved: A: RIGHT PARIETAL

Synoptic Report: Clinical Neuroimaging Findings: MRI: 2.1 x 2.1 cm right posterior parafalcine extra-axial enhancing mass Specimen Procedure: Resection Tumor Size: 3.0 Centimeters (cm) Tumor Tumor Site: Dura Tumor Laterality: Right Histologic Type: Meningioma Histologic Grade: WHO Grade 1 Best Tumor Blocks for Future Studies Tumor Block(s): B1, B2

CAP eCC 2022 Q1 Release

PATHOLOGIST COMMENT:

This meningothelial neoplasm is growing in fascicles and contains whorls and scattered psammoma bodies. The tumor cells are elongate with ovoid nuclei and a moderate amount of eosinophilic cytoplasm. Some tumor cells show prominent nucleoli. Up to one mitotic figure is identified in 10 high power fields.

Immunohistochemistry is performed on block B1 to characterize the tumor including CD3, Ki-67, and phosphohistone H3. CD3 labels scattered small T cells throughout the tumor. The Ki-67 is estimated as labeling approximately 1% of tumor cell nuclei. Phosphohistone H3 labels scattered mitotic figures.

**Electronically Signed Out on 6/28/2022 **

HILARY HIGHFIELD MD

If an analyte specific reagent was utilized, including certain Immunohistochemistry, these results are not, in and of themselves, diagnostic, but should be interpreted in the context of the clinical findings and the histopathologic diagnosis. This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by CPA Lab. It has not been cleared or approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. The FDA has determined that such clearance or approval is not necessary. This testis used for clinical purposes. It should not be regarded as investigational or for research. This laboratory is certified under the clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 (CLIA) as qualified to perform high-complexity clinical testing.

CLINICAL HISTORY: CLINICAL HISTORY AND SPECIAL REQUESTS MENINGIOMA

SPECIMEN SOURCE: A: RIGHT PARIETAL B: RIGHT PARIETAL

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

A. Received fresh for frozen section designated "right parietal" is an ovoid fragment of lobular tan-white soft tissue measuring 0.9 x 0.6 x 0.4 cm. Two touch preps are prepared and a representative portion of the specimen is submitted on a single block for frozen section. Following frozen section, specimen is submitted in cassette A1. All remaining tissue is submitted in cassette A2.

B. Received in formalin designated “right parietal” are multiple irregular fragments of pink-tan soft tissue measuring 3.0 x 2.0 x up to0.5cm. The specimens are entirely submitted in cassettes B1 and B2.

Admin instructions: Do Not Use Protocol If Serum Creatinine is Greater Than 2.0 and NOTIFY MD. *If Potassium is Greater Than 3.9 -AND- Sodium is Greater Than 145; NOTIFY MD for Phosphate Replacement Orders.

If Potassium is 4.0 or Greater, or Patient is Currently Being Given Potassium Chloride Runs: Sodium Phosphate Will Be Used.

*Check Phosphorus Level (and Potassium Level if Potassium Phosphate Administered) 2 Hrs Post Infusion and Repeat

Protocol if Necessary Up To a Maximum of 45 mmol Phosphate per Day Unless Specified Otherwise by MD Orders.

*Order Daily Phosphorus Level While Protocol is Active.

For Phosphate = 2 - 3 mg/dL Give 1 Dose. For Phosphate = 1.4 -1.9 mg/dL Give 2 Doses. For Phosphate = Less than 1.4 mg/dL Give 3 Doses. Package: 0409-7391-82, 0409-7923-23

Order comments: IF ON CRITICAL CARE POTASSIUM REPLACEMENT PROTOCOL: - Draw serum potassium level 2 hours after last supplemental dose of potassium chloride (Nursing enter orders when needed) and continue with KCL supplementation per potassium protocol. - If serum potassium level is less than 2.5 mmol/L, call MD. Also draw serum magnesium level and initiate MG protocol if serum MG level is less than 1.6 (Nursing enter orders when needed) IF ON CRITICAL CARE MAGNESIUM REPLACEMENT PROTOCOL: - Draw daily AM serum magnesium, potassium & creatinine levels if critical care Magnesium is given and continue with magnesium supplementation per protocol (Nursing enter orders as needed) - If magnesium level was less than 1.3 mg/dl, also draw serum magnesium and potassium levels 4 hours after last MG supplement and continue supplementation per MG protocol (Nursing enter orders as needed)

Questionnaire

Question Answer

Criteria for PRN ECG: acute chest pain / ACS, dysrhythmias or acute ST/T wave changes, respiratory or hemodynamic instability.

Nursing instructions: Enter ECG order in Epic EACH time ECG is done. Results will not cross over w/o an Epic order. Use "per active conditional order" mode.

Order comments: - If ECG positive for ST elevation, Notify attending MD and cardiologist (if applicable) STAT for STEMI 911. Ordering & Authorizing Provider Audit Trail

Question Answer

Criteria for PRN ECG: If initial EKG is not diagnostic but patient remains symptomatic and there is clinical suspicion for ACS, subsequent EKGs every 15 to 30 minutes for the first hour.

Nursing instructions: Enter ECG order in Epic EACH time ECG is done. Results will not cross over w/o an Epic order. Use "per active conditional order" mode.

Diagnoses

Meningioma [D32.9]

Order comments: Call the office with any questions - [PHONE-1634] Sutures will dissolve on their own If staples are in place they will be removed at your post-operative appointment Please shower daily starting on pod#2 If decadron is prescribed it will be slowly weaned over the course of 1-2 weeks, please be sure to take protonix while on the decadron Craniotomy Discharge Instructions Incision Care Wash your hair and gently massage the incision daily with baby shampoo. Do not use any creams or ointments. Do not blow dry, color or chemically treat your hair for at least 6 months. Discharge Activity Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment within 24 hours of using pain meds. If you have had seizures, do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until cleared by your surgeon. No lifting > 10 Ibs. (i.e. one gallon of milk) or strenuous activity until cleared by your surgeon. Light walking is encouraged. Discharge Diet Low sugar diet (No simple sugars, fruit juices, sweetened beverages, sweets made with sugar) while on steroids. Daily multi-vitamin with minerals. Vitamin C supplement (500 mg daily for 30 days, 250 mg daily thereafter), if you area current or recent smoker. Discharge Medications The nurses will give you a list of your new medications and home medication to resume. A limited supply of pain medication will be provided by your surgeon Take only as needed. Pain medication refills will only be considered by your surgeon after you have been seen on your scheduled routine follow-up visit. | have counseled the patient on the risks and benefits of narcotic use and patient has verbalized understanding. Prescription refills are completed during normal weekday office hours only. Please call the office at [PHONE-1635] for: A fever of greater than 101.5, measured with a thermometer. Swelling, tenderness, redness or drainage from your incision. Changes in level of awareness (restlessness, confusion, agitation, lethargy or increased sleepiness) New speech or swallowing problems New weakness, especially one-sided Changes in vision Persistent vomiting Severe headache, especially if worsening Seizures or tremors Leg swelling with calf tenderness If you think you are having an emergency, call 9-1-1. If possible, proceed to the hospital where your surgery was performed.

Meningioma [D32.9] Order comments: FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENT - Please call [PHONE-1637] to make and/or confirm your follow-up appointment. BRAIN SURGERY INCISION CARE - Wash your hair and gently massage the incision daily with baby shampoo. - Do not use any creams or ointments. - Do not blow dry, color or chemically treat your hair for at least 6 months. DISCHARGE ACTIVITY - Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment within 24 hours of using pain meds.

- If you have had seizures, do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until cleared by your surgeon. - No lifting > 10 Ibs. (i.e. one gallon of milk) or strenuous activity until cleared by your surgeon. - Light walking is encouraged - If you were issued a cervical collar, then wear it at all times - If you were issued a back brace, then wear it when sitting up greater than 45 degrees or standing DISCHARGE DIET - Low sugar diet (No simple sugars, fruit juices, sweetened beverages, sweets made with sugar) while on steroids. - Vitamin C supplement over the counter (500 mg daily for 30 days, 250 mg daily thereafter), if you are a current or recent smoker.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS - The nurses will give you a list of your new medications and home medication to resume. _—- A limited supply of pain medication will be provided by your surgeon Take only as needed. Pain medication refills will only be considered by your surgeon after you have been seen on your scheduled routine follow-up visit. | have counseled the patient on the risks and benefits of narcotic use and patient has verbalized understanding. _- Prescription refills are

completed during normal weekday office hours only. CALL THE OFFICE AT [PHONE-1638] FOR: - A fever of greater than 101.5 measured with a thermometer. _- Swelling, tenderness, redness or drainage from your incision. - Changes in level of awareness (restlessness, confusion, agitation, lethargy or increased sleepiness) - New speech or swallowing problems - New weakness, especially one-sided - Changes in vision - Persistent vomiting - Severe headache, especially if worsening _- Seizures or tremors - Leg swelling with calf tenderness - If you think you are having an emergency, call 9-1-1. If possible, proceed to the hospital where your surgery was performed. Indications

Goal: Remains infection free (Resolved)

Dates: Start: 06/22/22 Expected End: 06/25/22 Resolved: 06/23/22

Description: Assess and monitor vital signs, skin (color, moisture, integrity, turgor), respiratory status, urinary and gastrointestinal status, and labs (WBC, cultures). Administer antibiotics and antipyretics as ordered. Ensure aseptic care of all intravenous lines, invasive tubes/drains and wounds. Monitor for signs and symptoms of infection (redness, warmth, discharge, increased body temperature). Wash hands properly before and after each patient care activity. Follow isolation guidelines per hospital protocol/policy. Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as ordered.

[DOB-1800] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued)

Patient Education (continued)

Topic: Disease Management - PostOp General Surgery (Resolved)

Point: Incision/Wound Care (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for incision/wound to include: how to clean, when to clean and any special instruction for cleaning. Know how to care for your wound. Emphasize good hand hygiene before and after caring for the the wound. Teach about signs and symptoms of infection and when to notify healthcare provider. Teach to tell family and friends to never touch wound or bandage and to clean their hands before and after visiting. Instruct to keep incision/wound as clean and dry as possible. Caution against: scrubbing or rubbing the incision, removing the tape strips, using lotion or powder around the incision, exposing the incision to sunlight, taking a bath unless the incision can be kept dry.

Learning Progress Summary

Title: General Patient Education () Topic: Pain Management (Resolved)

Point: Pain Medication Actions & Side Effects (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver that pain medication can cause constipation. Explain that a stool softener might need to be given while taking pain medication.

Encourage patient to drink plenty of fluids and to eat fruits and vegetables.

Educate patient/family/caregiver to take pain medication with food to prevent nausea.

Provide medication specific handouts when available.

Patient Friendly Description: -IV Pain Medication Your nurse can call your doctor for an order and administer IV pain medication if indicated. This can be given through your IV and lasts 1-2 hours. This may make you feel sleepy or dizzy.

[DOB-1801] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued)

Patient Education (continued)

Point: Explain Pain Rating Scale (Resolved) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on the appropriate pain rating scale. Encourage patient/family/caregiver to monitor patient's pain.

Patient Friendly Description: -Your nurse will ask you to rate our pain on a scale of zero (0), for no pain, to ten (10), the worst pain you can experience.

-Your nurse will ask you to set a pain goal within zero (0) to ten (10) so that he or she can be sure that what we are doing is working.

-Mild pain is 1-3, Moderate pain is 4-6 and severe pain is 7-10. -We may not be able to take away ALL pain, so try to identify a point that pain would be manageable.

-So if your pain goal is 4 or less and you are rating your pain at 7, your nurse would give you a pain medication and then re-check your pain to ensure it is moving toward your pain goal of 4.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by GP at 6/22/2022 2000 Comment: Discussed pain control, care of incision that is no pet on bed when at home. Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by CF at 6/22/2022 1616 Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by CF at 6/22/2022 1616 Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by KD at 6/21/2022 1257

Point: Non-Pharmacological Comfort Measures (Resolved)

Description:

Explain to patient/family/caregiver that there are other ways of controlling pain than medication. The following are suggestions: position change, aromatherapy, deep slow breathing, distraction, quiet environment, imagery, heat therapy and/or cold therapy, laughter, massage, music, physical therapy, and touch therapy.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by GP at 6/22/2022 2000 Comment: Discussed pain control, care of incision that is no pet on bed when at home. Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by CF at 6/22/2022 1616 Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by CF at 6/22/2022 1616 Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back by KD at 6/21/2022 1257

Point: Patient Controlled Analgesia (Resolved)

Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver on the use of Patient Controlled Analgesia. Give the patient written information on Patient Controlled Analgesia. Explain how the pump works. Demonstrate pushing the button to give pain medicine to the patient. Caution the patient and other family/visitors that only the patient should press the PCA button for pain relief to decrease the chance of getting too much pain medicine.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by GP at 6/22/2022 2000 Comment: Discussed pain control, care of incision that is no pet on bed when at home. [DOB-1802] - Admission (Discharged) in NBH 3 West PCU (continued)

Patient Education (continued)

Point: Epidural Information (Resolved) Description: Educate the patient/family/caregiver on the use of Epidural Anaglesia. Explain why an epidural is used, how the epidural is placed and how the medication is given. If the epidural is PCA, demonstrate pushing the button to give pain medicine to the patient. Caution the patient and other family/visitors that only the patient should press the button for pain relief to decrease the chance of getting too much pain medicine. Written information available for reinforcement of education.

Patient Friendly Description: This is a procedure where an anesthesiologist or Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetist (CRNA) will place a catheter in your back that provides continuous medication during your labor to keep you comfortable.

-The anesthesiologist or CRNA will assess if you can have an epidural and will discuss risks of the procedure with you prior to placement.

-The epidural will make you feel numb from our waist down. It should take away sharp pain, but you will still feel pressure during labor. While pressure may be uncomfortable, it helps you know when to start pushing.

Craniotomy Discharge Instructions

Incision Care

Wash your hair and gently massage the incision daily with baby shampoo. Do not use any creams or ointments.

Do not blow dry, color or chemically treat your hair for at least 6 months.

Discharge Activity

Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment within 24 hours of using pain meds.

If you have had seizures, do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until cleared by your surgeon. No lifting > 10 Ibs. (i.e. one gallon of milk) or strenuous activity until cleared by your surgeon.

Light walking is encouraged.

Discharge Diet

Low sugar diet (No simple sugars, fruit juices, sweetened beverages, sweets made with sugar) while on steroids.

Daily multi-vitamin with minerals.

Vitamin C supplement (500 mg daily for 30 days, 250 mg daily thereafter), if you are a current or recent smoker.

Discharge Medications

The nurses will give you a list of your new medications and home medication to resume.

A limited supply of pain medication will be provided by your surgeon Take only as needed. Pain medication refills will only be considered by your surgeon after you have been seen on your scheduled routine follow-up visit. | have counseled the patient on the risks and benefits of narcotic use and patient has verbalized understanding.

Prescription refills are completed during normal weekday office hours only.

Please call the office at [PHONE-1811] for: A fever of greater than 101.5, measured with a thermometer.

Swelling, tenderness, redness or drainage from your incision. Changes in level of awareness (restlessness, confusion, agitation, lethargy or increased sleepiness)

Discharge instructions (specify) FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENT - Please call [PHONE-1813] to make and/or confirm your follow-up appointment.

BRAIN SURGERY INCISION CARE

- Wash your hair and gently massage the incision daily with baby shampoo. - Do not use any creams or ointments.

- Do not blow dry, color or chemically treat your hair for at least 6 months.

DISCHARGE ACTIVITY

- Do not drive or operate dangerous equipment within 24 hours of using pain meds.

- If you have had seizures, do not drive or operate dangerous equipment until cleared by your surgeon. - No lifting > 10 Ibs. (i.e. one gallon of milk) or strenuous activity until cleared by your surgeon.

- Light walking is encouraged

- If you were issued a cervical collar, then wear it at all times

- If you were issued a back brace, then wear it when sitting up greater than 45 degrees or standing

DISCHARGE DIET

- Low sugar diet (No simple sugars, fruit juices, sweetened beverages, sweets made with sugar) while on steroids.

- Vitamin C supplement over the counter (500 mg daily for 30 days, 250 mg daily thereafter), if you are a current or recent smoker.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS

- The nurses will give you a list of your new medications and home medication to resume.

- A limited supply of pain medication will be provided by your surgeon Take only as needed. Pain medication refills will only be considered by your surgeon after you have been seen on your scheduled routine follow-up visit. | have counseled the patient on the risks and benefits of narcotic use and patient has verbalized understanding.

- Prescription refills are completed during normal weekday office hours only.

CALL THE OFFICE AT [PHONE-1814] FOR:

- A fever of greater than 101.5 measured with a thermometer.

- Swelling, tenderness, redness or drainage from your incision.

- Changes in level of awareness (restlessness, confusion, agitation, lethargy or increased sleepiness) - New speech or swallowing problems

- New weakness, especially one-sided

- Changes in vision

- Persistent vomiting

- Severe headache, especially if worsening

- Seizures or tremors

- Leg swelling with calf tenderness

- If you think you are having an emergency, call 9-1-1. If possible, proceed to the hospital where your surgery was performed.

Incision / Wound care (specify) Check site often for bleeding, drainage, firmness, tenderness and/or swelling.

Keep dressing clean and dry Leave open to air

Medication Information for After Discharge

How to transfer refills on a prescription:

* If you have refills on a prescription and would like to fill them at a different pharmacy: take your bottle(s) with the prescription information to the pharmacy where you wish to have them filled. Or you can call the pharmacy where you wish to fill them and notify them you would like to transfer your prescription(s). You will need to provide them with information located on the bottle.

* To assure you do not miss doses, notify the pharmacy 2 to 3 days before you will run out.

How to get refills on medications that were started in the hospital:

* Itis common for doctors that discharge you from the hospital to only provide you with a short duration of medication(s).

* This does not mean that you do not need to stay on the medication(s), but they want you to be seen by your primary doctor to assess how you are doing and determine if you should stay on the same medication(s).

* It is very important that you have an appointment with your primary doctor within 30 days of discharge. At your appointment, be sure to take any new medications with you and ask them to provide you with new prescriptions if they feel you need to stay on them.

Safe medication storage and disposal

Medication Information for After Discharge (continued)

Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please visit

https://odcp.ky.gov

If Prescribed Narcotic Medications:

Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance.

While at Home: « Use medication only as directed. * Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect. Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours). Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider. Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.

Third Party Apps and Epic Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Your Same Day Care Options

We're committed to providing same-day care options, close to home, with the ease of online scheduling. Receive care with a Norton Healthcare provider in person at one of our primary care, Norton Immediate Care or Norton Prompt Care at Walgreens locations, or schedule a virtual visit with Norton eCare from the comfort of home. Access all of your care options at NortonNow.com.

Norton Care Call If you are going home after leaving a hospital overnight stay, you may receive a Norton Care Call.

¢ Within 48 hours of discharge, you may receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-1824] (Caller ID will read Norton Care Call)

* The automated call will ask you a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone keypad.

* If your responses indicate you need additional assistance, a Norton Healthcare team member will call you back to offer help and other instructions. Because our return calls are specific to your individual care, the returning call phone number will be from a different Norton phone number. Please answer this call so we can best support your health and progress.

I. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:

I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the facility. No guarantee has been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained. This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released when authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.

Il. OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:

I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician assistants) who are not employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students, and students of various health care fields may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees. When a physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III, V, and VI below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians, practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.

Ill. RELEASE OF INFORMATION:

I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s), of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as requested by such physician(s). I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me including medical information concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the completion of any claim for benefits arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). THE FACILITY IS NOT RESPONSIBLE FOR LOSS OF PERSONAL ARTICLES:

I understand that the facility maintains a safe for the safekeeping of money and valuables and the facility shall not be liable for the loss of or damage to

any money, jewelry, documents, or any other items of personal property, unless deposited in the facility safe for safekeeping. Furthermore, I accept full

responsibility for items of personal property kept in my possession.

V. ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

I agree to the assignment of all third-party benefits to the facility and agree to pay the facility for all charges not covered by third-party payors. In the

case of outpatient services I agree that this document shall remain in full force and effect until specifically revoked by me in writing. Any sums not paid

by a third-party payor are my obligation.

VI. RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:

I agree to the release by my insurance carrier to the facility and the facility's billing service of any eligibility and utilization data concerning my insurance

coverage which the facility and/or its billing supplier may require. I authorize the facility to file an appeal of a payment denial and/or request a hearing

regarding any denial of payment for treatment provided to me. I further authorize the facility to present proof, utilize facility records and my records

and documentation for appeals and hearings.

VII. PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:

I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and

terms of the facility. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its third-party

administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of bills are payable at the end of each week and in full

upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent accounts may be turned over to a collection agency and/or an

attorney for collection. Should it be necessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to pay the costs of collection,

including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account.

I agree that the facility, its employees, agents, attorneys and collection agencies, may call or text the cell number I provide for any reason, including but

not limited to inquiring about the status and collection of my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but

not limited to automatic dialing, artificial and prerecorded voice systems.

PATIENT INFORMATION TRANSFUSION OF BLOOD, BLOOD COMPONENTS & RH IMMUNE GLOBULIN

Consent: Your doctor may determine that a blood transfusion is needed as part of your medical care. It is important that you or your legal representative understand the risks and benefits of receiving a blood component transfusion. Your doctor will explain (or has explained) to you or your legal representative the risks and benefits of a blood component transfusion and any alternative treatment(s) to a blood transfusion. The following information on blood and blood components is being given to you or your legal representative to assist you or your legal representative in making an informed decision about receiving a blood transfusion. Before you or your legal representative sign the informed consent to receive a blood transfusion, your doctor or medical professional will be available to answer any questions you

may have about blood and blood transfusions. Please ask these individuals to explain anything that you did not understand about the risks and benefits of the blood transfusion you may require.

Benefits: Tests performed during your hospitalization may show that you need blood. Blood is made of many elements. Which elements you should get and the amount to be given are decisions made only by your doctor. * Red Blood cells - carry oxygen to your tissue and organs * Granulocytes - white cells needed to fight infection | * Platelets - help clot your blood * Cryoprecipitate -Several proteins needed to clot your blood * Plasma - liquid portion assists in clotting your blood Testing the blood you receive: Volunteer donors, that undergo strict screening, donate blood. Many tests (in addition to the blood type) are performed on this blood and are tested by the blood supplier: * Syphilis (a sexually transmitted disease) * Hepatitis B and C (viruses that injure the liver) * HIV (viruses that cause AIDS) * HTLV (human T-cell lymphotrophic viruses which may cause blood and nerve disease) * WNV West Nile Virus (virus that may cause Encephalitis) Any blood donation found to be positive for any of these tests is destroyed. Finally, if you are going to receive red blood cells, the hospital blood bank will test a sample of your blood and that of the acceptable donated blood unit to make sure that the blood is compatible crossmatching your blood). Risks. Blood should be thought of as a drug having certain risks or side effects. Some patients may have a mild reaction to a blood transfusion such as fever, chills, hives or body aches. More severe reactions such as breathing difficulties and shock occur very rarely. Human error, although uncommon, may also occur during preparation of your blood component for transfusion. The risk of getting an infectious disease such as hepatitis or HIV from a blood transfusion is very rare.

Alternative Treatments:

* Autologous blood transfusion refers to procedures in which you may serve as your own blood donor. In some cases, this may be done prior to your hospitalization. Autologous units are reserved for your use and kept until the expiration date if not used by you. They cannot ever be given to another person if not used by you. If this blood is insufficient for your transfusion needs, then blood components from other donors will be used.

* In other cases, blood lost during surgery can be washed and given back to you, (called intraoperative cell salvage or cell saver). Those with religious objections can opt for a cell saver with a continuous closed circuit.

* In other situations, you may be able to select the donors of your blood (family or friends) and receive a directed donation. Studies have shown, however, that these donations are no safer than that from the community blood supply. Directed units that you do not use will be released on the day of your hospital discharge for use by other patients unless your physician notifies the blood bank to hold those units for your use in the future or until the expiration date.

* Bloodless medicine/surgery techniques (called Blood Conservation) may be appropriate for selected patients in certain circumstances.

TO THE PATIENT: Anesthesia is necessary to facilitate the recommended surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) you have consented to undergo and the use of invasive monitors (which are devices that enter the body to aid the physician in making decisions regarding a patient’s vital functions) may be necessary to assist with the provision of the anesthesia. You have the right, as a patient, to be informed about the provision of anesthesia and invasive monitors in connection with the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) to be used to diagnose or treat your condition.

| understand that, in connection with the surgical, medical, or diagnostic procedure(s) used to diagnose or treat my condition, the physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists of Professional Anesthesia Services of Kentucky, PLLC, (my “Anesthesia Provider(s)”) and such associates, technical assistants and other health care providers as they may deem necessary, will be the providers of the anesthesia services and invasive monitors, if necessary.

A physician who is not affiliated with Professional Anesthesia Services of Kentucky, PLLC (my “Treating Physician’), has already explained to me what my condition is, and | have already consented to the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) recommended by my Treating Physician to diagnose or treat my condition.

l understand that my Treating Physician and/or Anesthesia Provider may discover other or different conditions which require additional or different procedures than those planned. | understand that the type of anesthesia service may have to be changed, possibly without explanation to me. | authorize the Anesthesia Providers to provide such other anesthesia services which are advisable in their professional judgment.

RISKS AND HAZARDS ASSOCIATED WITH ANESTHESIA:

| understand that all forms of anesthesia involve some risks and no guarantees or promises can be made concerning the results of my procedure or treatment. Just as there may be risks and hazards in continuing my present condition without treatment, there are also risks and hazards related to the performance of the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s), including the anesthesia planned for me. | realize that severe complications with anesthesia can occur, including the potential for infection, blood clots in veins and lungs, hemorrhage, drug reactions, respiratory problems, cardiac/cardiovascular/other major organ problems, loss of limb function or sensation, paralysis, stroke, brain damage and even death. | further understand that, in addition to the complications noted above, the following risks and hazards may occur in connection with the forms of anesthesia listed below:

GENERAL ANESTHESIA Description: Total unconscious state, possible placement of a tube into the windpipe Technique: Drug injected into the bloodstream, breathed into the lungs, or administered by other routes Risks: Permanent organ damage; memory dysfunction/memory loss; minor discomfort; mouth, jaw or throat pain; hoarseness; injury to mouth, teeth, lips, gums, vocal cords, or eyes; need for post-operative ventilation; awareness under anesthesia; and brain damage.

0 EPIDURAL / SPINAL Description: Temporary decrease or loss of feeling and/or movement to lower part of body Technique: Drug injected through a needle/catheter placed either directly into the spinal canal or immediately outside the spinal canal Risks: Headache; chronic pain; persistent back pain; bleeding/epidural hematoma; infection; nerve injury or damage; “total spinal”; potential to convert to a general anesthetic if the block fails or the procedure outlasts the block; and/or brain damage.

co MONITORED ANESTHESIA CARE WITH SEDATION (MAC or Conscious Sedation) Description: Reduced anxiety and pain, partial or total amnesia Technique: Drug injected into the bloodstream, breathed into the lungs, or administered by other routes producing a semi- conscious state. This may include the administration of drugs to help alleviate anxiety, either alone or as a supplement to a local or regional technique. Risks: Permanent organ damage; brain damage; memory dysfunction/memory loss; potential to convert to general anesthetic if the sedation is not adequate; an unconscious state; and/or difficulty with breathing. Please know that MAC is a vigilant monitoring of a patient’s vital functions while undergoing therapeutic or diagnostic procedures.
